([𡨸喃](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%F0%A1%A8%B8%E5%96%83 "wikt:𡨸喃"), IPA: [[t͡ɕɯ˦ˀ˥ nom˧˧]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Vietnamese "Help:IPA/Vietnamese"))[[5]](#cite_note-5) is a [logographic](/wiki/Logogram "Logogram") writing system formerly used to write the [Vietnamese language](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language"). It uses [Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_characters "Chinese characters") to represent [Sino-Vietnamese vocabulary](/wiki/Sino-Vietnamese_vocabulary "Sino-Vietnamese vocabulary") and some native Vietnamese words, with other words represented by new characters created using a variety of methods, including [phono-semantic compounds](/wiki/Phono-semantic_compound "Phono-semantic compound").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi2020102–103-6) This composite script was therefore highly complex and was accessible to the less than five percent of the Vietnamese population who had mastered written Chinese.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199782–83-7)

**Chữ Nôm** ([𡨸喃](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%F0%A1%A8%B8%E5%96%83 "wikt:𡨸喃"), IPA: [[t͡ɕɯ˦ˀ˥ nom˧˧]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Vietnamese "Help:IPA/Vietnamese"))[[5]](#cite_note-5) is a [logographic](/wiki/Logogram "Logogram") writing system formerly used to write the [Vietnamese language](/wiki/Vietnamese_language "Vietnamese language"). It uses [Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_characters "Chinese characters") to represent [Sino-Vietnamese vocabulary](/wiki/Sino-Vietnamese_vocabulary "Sino-Vietnamese vocabulary") and some native Vietnamese words, with other words represented by new characters created using a variety of methods, including [phono-semantic compounds](/wiki/Phono-semantic_compound "Phono-semantic compound").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi2020102–103-6) This composite script was therefore highly complex and was accessible to the less than five percent of the Vietnamese population who had mastered written Chinese.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199782–83-7)

Although all formal writing in [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") was done in [Classical Chinese](/wiki/Classical_Chinese "Classical Chinese") until the early 20th century (except for two brief interludes),[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFrancis197732,_38-8) between the 15th and 19th centuries some Vietnamese literati used chữ Nôm to create popular works in the vernacular, many in verse. One of the best-known pieces of [Vietnamese literature](/wiki/Vietnamese_literature "Vietnamese literature"), *[The Tale of Kiều](/wiki/The_Tale_of_Ki%E1%BB%81u "The Tale of Kiều")*, was written in chữ Nôm by [Nguyễn Du](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Du "Nguyễn Du").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The [Vietnamese alphabet](/wiki/Vietnamese_alphabet "Vietnamese alphabet") created by Portuguese [Jesuit](/wiki/Jesuit "Jesuit") missionaries, with the earliest known usage occurring in the 17th century, replaced chữ Nôm as the preferred way to record Vietnamese literature from the 1920s. While Chinese characters are still used for decorative, historic and ceremonial value, chữ Nôm has fallen out of mainstream use in modern Vietnam. In the 21st century, chữ Nôm is being used in Vietnam for historical and [liturgical](/wiki/Sacred_language "Sacred language") purposes. The [Institute of Hán-Nôm Studies](/wiki/Institute_of_H%C3%A1n-N%C3%B4m_Studies "Institute of Hán-Nôm Studies") at [Hanoi](/wiki/Hanoi "Hanoi") is the main research centre for pre-modern texts from Vietnam, both [Chinese-language texts written in Chinese characters](/wiki/Literary_Chinese_in_Vietnam "Literary Chinese in Vietnam") (*chữ Hán*) and Vietnamese-language texts in chữ Nôm.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The Vietnamese word *chữ* 'character' is derived from the [Middle Chinese](/wiki/Middle_Chinese "Middle Chinese") word *dziH* [字](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%AD%97#Chinese "wikt:字"), meaning '[Chinese] character'.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFrancis197726-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11) The word *Nôm* 'Southern' is derived from the [Middle Chinese](/wiki/Middle_Chinese "Middle Chinese") word *nom* [南](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%8D%97#Chinese "wikt:南"),[[a]](#cite_note-12) meaning 'south'.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFrancis197727-13)[[13]](#cite_note-14) It could also be based on the dialectal pronunciation from the South Central dialects (most notably in the name of province of *[Quảng Nam](/wiki/Qu%E1%BA%A3ng_Nam_province "Quảng Nam province")*, known locally as *Quảng Nôm*).[[14]](#cite_note-15)

There are many ways to write the name *chữ Nôm* in chữ Nôm characters. The word *chữ* may be written as 字, 𫳘(⿰字宁), 𡨸, 𫿰(⿰字文), 𡦂(⿰字字), 𲂯(⿰貝字), 𱚂(⿱字渚), or 宁, while *Nôm* is written as 喃.[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17)

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Terminology")]

Main article: [History of writing in Vietnam](/wiki/History_of_writing_in_Vietnam "History of writing in Vietnam")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Cover_page_of_%C4%90%E1%BA%A1i_Nam_qu%E1%BB%91c_ng%E1%BB%AF_%E5%A4%A7%E5%8D%97%E5%9C%8B%E8%AA%9E.png/250px-Cover_page_of_%C4%90%E1%BA%A1i_Nam_qu%E1%BB%91c_ng%E1%BB%AF_%E5%A4%A7%E5%8D%97%E5%9C%8B%E8%AA%9E.png)](/wiki/File:Cover_page_of_%C4%90%E1%BA%A1i_Nam_qu%E1%BB%91c_ng%E1%BB%AF_%E5%A4%A7%E5%8D%97%E5%9C%8B%E8%AA%9E.png)

*Quốc ngữ* (國語) was used historically to refer to *chữ Nôm*. Such as in the book, *Đại Nam quốc ngữ* (大南國語), a Literary Chinese – Vietnamese (*chữ Nôm*) dictionary.

*Chữ Nôm* is the logographic writing system of the Vietnamese language. It is based on the Chinese writing system but adds a large number of new characters to make it fit the Vietnamese language. Common historical terms for chữ Nôm were *Quốc Âm* (國音, 'national sound') and *Quốc ngữ* (國語, 'national language').

In Vietnamese, [Chinese characters](/wiki/Chinese_characters "Chinese characters") are called *chữ Hán* ([𡨸漢](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%F0%A1%A8%B8%E6%BC%A2 "wikt:𡨸漢") 'Han characters'), *chữ Nho* ([𡨸儒](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%F0%A1%A8%B8%E5%84%92 "wikt:𡨸儒") 'Confucian characters', due to the connection with [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism")) and uncommonly as *Hán tự* ([漢字](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%BC%A2%E5%AD%97 "wikt:漢字") 'Han characters').[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20) *Hán văn* ([漢文](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%BC%A2%E6%96%87 "wikt:漢文")) refers literature written in Literary Chinese.[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22)

The term *Hán Nôm* ([漢喃](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%BC%A2%E5%96%83 "wikt:漢喃") 'Han and chữ Nôm characters')[[22]](#cite_note-23) in Vietnamese designates the whole body of premodern written materials from Vietnam, either written in Chinese (*chữ Hán*) or in Vietnamese (*chữ Nôm*).[[23]](#cite_note-24) Hán and Nôm could also be found in the same document side by side,[[24]](#cite_note-25) for example, in the case of translations of books on [Chinese medicine](/wiki/Chinese_medicine "Chinese medicine").[[25]](#cite_note-26) The [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist") history *Cổ Châu Pháp Vân phật bản hạnh ngữ lục* (1752) gives the story of early [Buddhism in Vietnam](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Vietnam "Buddhism in Vietnam") both in Hán script and in a parallel Nôm translation.[[26]](#cite_note-27) The Jesuit [Girolamo Maiorica](/wiki/Girolamo_Maiorica "Girolamo Maiorica") (1605–1656) had also used parallel Hán and Nôm texts.

The term *chữ Quốc ngữ* ([𡨸](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%F0%A1%A8%B8 "wikt:𡨸")[國](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%9C%8B "wikt:國")[語](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%AA%9E "wikt:語") 'national language script') refers to the [Vietnamese alphabet](/wiki/Vietnamese_alphabet "Vietnamese alphabet") in current use, but was used to refer to chữ Nôm before the Vietnamese alphabet was widely used.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Tu_duc_thanh_che_tu_hoc_giai_nghia_ca.jpg/250px-Tu_duc_thanh_che_tu_hoc_giai_nghia_ca.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tu_duc_thanh_che_tu_hoc_giai_nghia_ca.jpg)

A page from *[Tự Đức thánh chế tự học giải nghĩa ca](/wiki/T%E1%BB%B1_%C4%90%E1%BB%A9c_th%C3%A1nh_ch%E1%BA%BF_t%E1%BB%B1_h%E1%BB%8Dc_gi%E1%BA%A3i_ngh%C4%A9a_ca "Tự Đức thánh chế tự học giải nghĩa ca")* (嗣德聖製字學解義歌), a 19th-century primer for teaching Vietnamese children Chinese characters. The work is attributed to Emperor [Tự Đức](/wiki/T%E1%BB%B1_%C4%90%E1%BB%A9c "Tự Đức"), the fourth emperor of the Nguyễn dynasty. In this primer, chữ Nôm is used to gloss the Chinese characters, for example, 𡗶 is used to gloss 天.

Chinese characters were introduced to Vietnam after the [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") [conquered](/wiki/Han_conquest_of_Nanyue "Han conquest of Nanyue") [Nanyue](/wiki/Nanyue "Nanyue") in 111 BC. Independence was achieved after the [Battle of Bạch Đằng](/wiki/Battle_of_B%E1%BA%A1ch_%C4%90%E1%BA%B1ng_(938) "Battle of Bạch Đằng (938)") in 938, but [Literary Chinese](/wiki/Literary_Chinese "Literary Chinese") was [adopted](/wiki/Adoption_of_Chinese_literary_culture#Vietnam "Adoption of Chinese literary culture") for official purposes in 1010.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199778–79,_82-28) For most of the period up to the early 20th century, formal writing was indistinguishable from contemporaneous classical Chinese works produced in China, Korea, and [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan").[[28]](#cite_note-Marr141-29)

Vietnamese scholars were thus intimately familiar with Chinese writing. In order to record their native language, they applied the structural principles of Chinese characters to develop chữ Nôm. The new script was mostly used to record folk songs and for other popular literature.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarr1984141-30) Vietnamese written in chữ Nôm briefly replaced Chinese for official purposes under the [Hồ dynasty](/wiki/H%E1%BB%93_dynasty "Hồ dynasty") (1400–1407) and under the [Tây Sơn](/wiki/T%C3%A2y_S%C6%A1n "Tây Sơn") (1778–1802), but in both cases this was swiftly reversed.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFrancis197732,_38-8)

### Early development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early development")]

Further information: [List of early inscriptions in Vietnam](/wiki/List_of_early_inscriptions_in_Vietnam "List of early inscriptions in Vietnam")

The use of Chinese characters to transcribe the Vietnamese language can be traced to an inscription with the two characters "[布](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%B8%83 "wikt:布")[蓋](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%93%8B "wikt:蓋")", as part of the posthumous title of [Phùng Hưng](/wiki/Ph%C3%B9ng_H%C6%B0ng "Phùng Hưng"), a national hero who succeeded in briefly expelling the Chinese in the late 8th century. The two characters have literal Chinese meanings 'cloth' and 'cover', which make no sense in this context. They have thus been interpreted as a phonetic transcription, via their [Middle Chinese](/wiki/Middle_Chinese "Middle Chinese") pronunciations *buH kajH*, of a Vietnamese phrase, either *vua cái* 'great king', or *bố cái* 'father and mother' (of the people).[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFrancis197721–22-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32)

After Vietnam established its independence from China in the 10th century, [Đinh Bộ Lĩnh](/wiki/Dinh_Bo_Linh "Dinh Bo Linh") (r. 968–979), the founder of the [Đinh dynasty](/wiki/%C4%90inh_dynasty "Đinh dynasty"), named the country *Đại Cồ Việt* [大](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A4%A7 "wikt:大")[瞿](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%9E%BF "wikt:瞿")[越](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%B6%8A "wikt:越"). The first and third Chinese characters mean 'great' and 'Viet'. The second character was often used to transcribe non-Chinese terms and names phonetically. In this context, *cồ* is an obsolete Vietnamese word for 'big'.[[b]](#cite_note-33)[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFrancis197722–23-34)[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKiernan2017141-35)

The oldest surviving inscription using Chinese characters to transcribe Vietnamese names is on a stele at the Báo Ân pagoda in Tháp Miếu village ([Phúc Yên](/wiki/Ph%C3%BAc_Y%C3%AAn "Phúc Yên"), [Vĩnh Phúc province](/wiki/V%C4%A9nh_Ph%C3%BAc_province "Vĩnh Phúc province")). The inscription, re-engraved in the 18th century from an original dating from 1209, is written in Chinese but includes names of 21 people and villages written in an early form of Nom.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFrancis197723–24-36)[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKiernan2017138-37)[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENguyễn1990395-38)[[37]](#cite_note-39) Another stele at Hộ Thành Sơn in [Ninh Bình Province](/wiki/Ninh_B%C3%ACnh_Province "Ninh Bình Province") (1343) is reported as listing 20 villages.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFrancis197723-40)[[39]](#cite_note-41)[[c]](#cite_note-43)

[Trần Nhân Tông](/wiki/Tran_Nhan_Tong "Tran Nhan Tong") (r. 1278–1293) ordered that Nôm be used to communicate his proclamations to the people.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFrancis197723-40)[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi2020104-44)
The first literary writing in Vietnamese is said to have been an [incantation](/wiki/Incantation "Incantation") in verse composed in 1282 by the Minister of Justice [Nguyễn Thuyên](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Thuy%C3%AAn "Nguyễn Thuyên") and thrown into the [Red River](/wiki/Red_River_(Asia) "Red River (Asia)") to expel a menacing [crocodile](/wiki/Crocodile "Crocodile").[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFrancis197723-40)
Four poems written in Nom from the Tran dynasty, two by Trần Nhân Tông and one each by [Huyền Quang](/wiki/Huy%E1%BB%81n_Quang "Huyền Quang") and [Mạc Đĩnh Chi](/wiki/M%E1%BA%A1c_%C4%90%C4%A9nh_Chi "Mạc Đĩnh Chi"), were collected and published in 1805.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENguyễn1990396-45)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Page_from_Ph%E1%BA%ADt_thuy%E1%BA%BFt_%C4%91%E1%BA%A1i_b%C3%A1o_ph%E1%BB%A5_m%E1%BA%ABu_%C3%A2n_tr%E1%BB%8Dng_kinh_%E4%BD%9B%E8%AA%AA%E5%A4%A7%E5%A0%B1%E7%88%B6%E6%AF%8D%E6%81%A9%E9%87%8D%E7%B6%93_showing_consonant_cluster.png/250px-Page_from_Ph%E1%BA%ADt_thuy%E1%BA%BFt_%C4%91%E1%BA%A1i_b%C3%A1o_ph%E1%BB%A5_m%E1%BA%ABu_%C3%A2n_tr%E1%BB%8Dng_kinh_%E4%BD%9B%E8%AA%AA%E5%A4%A7%E5%A0%B1%E7%88%B6%E6%AF%8D%E6%81%A9%E9%87%8D%E7%B6%93_showing_consonant_cluster.png)](/wiki/File:Page_from_Ph%E1%BA%ADt_thuy%E1%BA%BFt_%C4%91%E1%BA%A1i_b%C3%A1o_ph%E1%BB%A5_m%E1%BA%ABu_%C3%A2n_tr%E1%BB%8Dng_kinh_%E4%BD%9B%E8%AA%AA%E5%A4%A7%E5%A0%B1%E7%88%B6%E6%AF%8D%E6%81%A9%E9%87%8D%E7%B6%93_showing_consonant_cluster.png)

A page of *Phật thuyết đại báo phụ mẫu ân trọng kinh* (佛說大報父母恩重經) shows text in Literary Chinese alongside an earlier form of chữ Nôm recording Old Vietnamese. Some characters are double-phonetic characters (*hội âm*; 會音)[[43]](#cite_note-46) used to represent the consonant clusters and polysyllabic words that were present in Old Vietnamese.[[44]](#cite_note-47) In the second column, the first character is one such example. *Blời* (*trời*) is written as 𫶸 where 巴 *ba* represents ⟨b⟩ and 例 *lệ* represents ⟨l⟩ in ⟨bl⟩.

The Nôm text *Phật thuyết đại báo phụ mẫu ân trọng kinh* ('Sūtra explained by the Buddha on the Great Repayment of the Heavy Debt to Parents') was printed around 1730, but conspicuously avoids the character [利](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%88%A9 "wikt:利") *lợi*, suggesting that it was written (or copied) during the reign of [Lê Lợi](/wiki/L%C3%AA_L%E1%BB%A3i "Lê Lợi") (1428–1433).
Based on archaic features of the text compared with the Tran dynasty poems, including an exceptional number of words with initial consonant clusters written with pairs of characters, some scholars suggest that it is a copy of an earlier original, perhaps as early as the 12th century.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGong201960-48)

### Hồ dynasty (1400–1407) and Ming conquest (1407–1427)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Hồ dynasty (1400–1407) and Ming conquest (1407–1427)")]

During the seven years of the [Hồ dynasty](/wiki/H%E1%BB%93_dynasty "Hồ dynasty") (1400–1407) Classical Chinese was discouraged in favor of vernacular Vietnamese written in Nôm, which became the official script. The emperor [Hồ Quý Ly](/wiki/H%E1%BB%93_Qu%C3%BD_Ly "Hồ Quý Ly") even ordered the translation of the [Book of Documents](/wiki/Book_of_Documents "Book of Documents") into Nôm and pushed for reinterpretation of Confucian thoughts in his book *Minh đạo*.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi2020104-44) These efforts were reversed with the fall of the Hồ and [Chinese conquest of 1407](/wiki/Fourth_Chinese_domination "Fourth Chinese domination"), lasting twenty years, during which use of the vernacular language and demotic script were suppressed.[[46]](#cite_note-49)

During the [Ming dynasty occupation of Vietnam](/wiki/Ming_dynasty_occupation_of_Vietnam "Ming dynasty occupation of Vietnam"), chữ Nôm printing blocks, texts and inscriptions were thoroughly destroyed; as a result the earliest surviving texts of chữ Nôm post-date the occupation.[[47]](#cite_note-50)

### 15th to 19th centuries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 15th to 19th centuries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Nh%E1%BA%ADt_d%E1%BB%A5ng_th%C6%B0%E1%BB%9Dng_%C4%91%C3%A0m%2C_p._38.jpg/250px-Nh%E1%BA%ADt_d%E1%BB%A5ng_th%C6%B0%E1%BB%9Dng_%C4%91%C3%A0m%2C_p._38.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nh%E1%BA%ADt_d%E1%BB%A5ng_th%C6%B0%E1%BB%9Dng_%C4%91%C3%A0m,_p._38.jpg)

A page from the bilingual dictionary *Nhật dụng thường đàm* (日用常談; 1851). Characters representing words in *Hán* (Chinese) are explained in *Nôm* (Vietnamese).

Chữ Nôm was not reinstated as the official script after the demise of the [Hồ dynasty](/wiki/H%E1%BB%93_dynasty "Hồ dynasty") and restoration of Viet rule.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBianco2013175-51) Very few extant vernacular texts in Nôm predate the 15th century and even many later texts in Nôm were translations or rewritings of works in Chinese. During the 15th and 16th centuries, reformist governments translated [Chinese Classics](/wiki/Chinese_Classics "Chinese Classics") into Nôm, but these translations have not survived due to being seen as subversive by successive governments. As a result of its marginalized nature and lack of institutional backing, chữ Nôm was used as a medium for social protest during the [Lê dynasty](/wiki/L%C3%AA_dynasty "Lê dynasty") (1428–1789), leading to its banning in 1663, 1718, and 1760.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199778–84-52)

While almost all official writings and documents continued to be written in [Classical Chinese](/wiki/Classical_Chinese "Classical Chinese") until the early 20th century, Nôm was used for popular literary compositions. The corpus of Nôm writings grew over time as did more scholarly compilations of the script itself. Among the earlier works in Nôm of this era are the writings of [Nguyễn Trãi](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Tr%C3%A3i "Nguyễn Trãi") (1380–1442) which aimed to disseminate [Neo-Confucianism](/wiki/Neo-Confucianism "Neo-Confucianism") among as broad an audience as possible.[[50]](#cite_note-McLeod-53) [Trịnh Thị Ngọc Trúc](/w/index.php?title=Tr%E1%BB%8Bnh_Th%E1%BB%8B_Ng%E1%BB%8Dc_Tr%C3%BAc&action=edit&redlink=1 "Trịnh Thị Ngọc Trúc (page does not exist)") [[vi](https://vi.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tr%E1%BB%8Bnh_Th%E1%BB%8B_Ng%E1%BB%8Dc_Tr%C3%BAc "vi:Trịnh Thị Ngọc Trúc")], consort of King [Lê Thần Tông](/wiki/L%C3%AA_Th%E1%BA%A7n_T%C3%B4ng "Lê Thần Tông"), is generally given credit for **[Chỉ nam ngọc âm giải nghĩa](/w/index.php?title=Ch%E1%BB%89_nam_ng%E1%BB%8Dc_%C3%A2m_gi%E1%BA%A3i_ngh%C4%A9a&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chỉ nam ngọc âm giải nghĩa (page does not exist)") [[vi](https://vi.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ch%E1%BB%89_nam_ng%E1%BB%8Dc_%C3%A2m_gi%E1%BA%A3i_ngh%C4%A9a "vi:Chỉ nam ngọc âm giải nghĩa")]** (指南玉音解義; 'guide to Southern Jade sounds: explanations and meanings'), a 24,000-character bilingual Hán-to-Nôm [dictionary](/wiki/Dictionary "Dictionary") compiled between the 15th and 18th centuries, most likely in 1641 or 1761.[[51]](#cite_note-chu-54)[[52]](#cite_note-Phan_2013-55)

The [Tây Sơn dynasty](/wiki/T%C3%A2y_S%C6%A1n_dynasty "Tây Sơn dynasty") (1778–1802) mandated the use of Nôm in both government business and civil service examinations but their policy was reverted after the dynasty's collapse. [Gia Long](/wiki/Gia_Long "Gia Long") (r. 1802–1820), founder of the [Nguyễn dynasty](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_dynasty "Nguyễn dynasty") (1802–1945), supported chữ Nôm only for as long as it took for him to become emperor and then immediately reverted to Chinese. His successor, [Minh Mạng](/wiki/Minh_M%E1%BA%A1ng "Minh Mạng") (r. 1820–1841), the second emperor of the Nguyễn dynasty, prohibited the use of Nôm in the government.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKornicki2017569-570-56)[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199778–84-52)

Nôm reached its golden period with the Nguyễn dynasty in the 19th century as it became a vehicle for diverse genres, from novels to theatrical pieces, and instructional manuals.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKornicki2017570-57) Apogees of Vietnamese literature emerged with [Nguyễn Du](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Du "Nguyễn Du")'s *[The Tale of Kiều](/wiki/The_Tale_of_Ki%E1%BB%81u "The Tale of Kiều")*[[55]](#cite_note-58) and [Hồ Xuân Hương](/wiki/H%E1%BB%93_Xu%C3%A2n_H%C6%B0%C6%A1ng "Hồ Xuân Hương")'s poetry. Although literacy in premodern Vietnam was limited to just 3 to 5 percent of the population,[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199778-59) nearly every village had someone who could read Nôm aloud for the benefit of other villagers.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarr1984142-60) Thus these Nôm works circulated orally in the villages, and were accessible even to the illiterates.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFrancis197744–46-61)

Nôm was used as the dominant script in [Vietnamese Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Vietnam "Catholic Church in Vietnam") literature until the late 19th century.[[59]](#cite_note-62) In 1838, [Jean-Louis Taberd](/wiki/Jean-Louis_Taberd "Jean-Louis Taberd") compiled a Nôm dictionary, helping with the standardization of the script.[[60]](#cite_note-Taberd-63) The reformist Catholic scholar [Nguyễn Trường Tộ](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_Tr%C6%B0%E1%BB%9Dng_T%E1%BB%99 "Nguyễn Trường Tộ") presented the Emperor [Tự Đức](/wiki/T%E1%BB%B1_%C4%90%E1%BB%A9c "Tự Đức") with a series of unsuccessful petitions (written in Classical Chinese, like all court documents) proposing reforms in several areas of government and society. His petition *Tế cấp bát điều* (濟急八條 'Eight urgent matters', 1867), includes proposals on education, including a section entitled *Xin khoan dung quốc âm* ('Please tolerate the national voice'). He proposed to replace Classical Chinese with Vietnamese written using a script based on Chinese characters that he called *Quốc âm Hán tự* (國音漢字 'Han characters with national pronunciations'), though he described this as a new creation, and did not mention chữ Nôm.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFrancis1977101–105-64)[[62]](#cite_note-65)[[63]](#cite_note-66)

Despite the increasing use of Nôm for popular literature and as a medium for oral dissemination in rural areas, this composite script was never able to supplant Classical Chinese as the primary script of Vietnam. It was highly complex and inefficient, requiring the user to have some prior knowledge of the Chinese script. It was accessible to less than five percent of the Vietnamese population who had already mastered written Chinese and served primarily as a way to learn Classical Chinese and to record folk literature.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199782–83-7) The small number of literati who took Nôm seriously had to contend with their peers and make sure not to offend their sense of propriety. Even figures known to have written in Nôm such as [Lê Thánh Tông](/wiki/L%C3%AA_Th%C3%A1nh_T%C3%B4ng "Lê Thánh Tông") and Nguyễn Trãi were more renowned for their Chinese language writings.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarr1984142–143-67) According to language researcher Nguyen Thuy Dan, the majority of the Vietnamese elite up to the 19th century seem to have never written in anything but Classical Chinese and even criticized the use of Nôm.[[65]](#cite_note-68)

### French Indochina and the Latin alphabet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: French Indochina and the Latin alphabet")]

From the latter half of the 19th century onwards, the [French colonial authorities](/wiki/Cochinchina "Cochinchina") discouraged or simply banned the use of classical [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"), and promoted the use of the Vietnamese alphabet, which they viewed as a stepping stone toward learning French. Language reform movements in other Asian nations stimulated Vietnamese interest in the subject. Following the [Russo-Japanese War](/wiki/Russo-Japanese_War "Russo-Japanese War") of 1905, Japan was increasingly cited as a model for modernization. The Confucian education system was compared unfavourably to the Japanese system of public education. According to a polemic by writer [Phan Châu Trinh](/wiki/Phan_Ch%C3%A2u_Trinh "Phan Châu Trinh"), "so-called Confucian scholars" lacked knowledge of the modern world, as well as real understanding of Han literature. Their degrees showed only that they had learned how to write characters, he claimed.[[66]](#cite_note-69)

The popularity of Hanoi's short-lived [Tonkin Free School](/wiki/Tonkin_Free_School "Tonkin Free School") suggested that broad reform was possible. In 1910, the colonial school system adopted a "Franco-Vietnamese curriculum", which emphasized French and alphabetic Vietnamese. The traditional Civil Service Examination, which emphasized the command of classical Chinese, was dismantled in 1915 in [Tonkin](/wiki/Tonkin "Tonkin") and was given for the last time at the imperial capital of [Huế](/wiki/Hu%E1%BA%BF "Huế") on January 4, 1919.[[67]](#cite_note-Chung-70) The examination system, and the education system based on it, had been in effect for almost 900 years.[[67]](#cite_note-Chung-70)

The decline of the Chinese script also led to the decline of chữ Nôm given that Nôm and Chinese characters are so intimately connected.[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFrancis1977179-71) After the First World War, chữ Nôm gradually died out as the Vietnamese alphabet grew more and popular.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFrancis1977205-72) In an article published in 1935 (based on a lecture given in 1925), Georges Cordier estimated that 70% of literate persons knew the alphabet, 20% knew chữ Nôm and 10% knew Chinese characters.[[70]](#cite_note-Cordier-73)
However, estimates of the literacy rate in the late 1930s range from 5% to 20%.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFrancis1977218-74)
By 1953, literacy (using the alphabet) had risen to 70%.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDeFrancis1977240-75)

The [Gin people](/wiki/Gin_people "Gin people"), descendants of 16th-century migrants from Vietnam to islands off [Dongxing](/wiki/Dongxing,_Guangxi "Dongxing, Guangxi") in southern [China](/wiki/China "China"), now speak a form of [Yue Chinese](/wiki/Yue_Chinese "Yue Chinese") and Vietnamese, but their priests use songbooks and scriptures written in chữ Nôm in their ceremonies.[[73]](#cite_note-76)

### Types of texts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Types of texts")]

* *[Giải âm](/wiki/Gi%E1%BA%A3i_%C3%A2m "Giải âm")* (解音) – a category of chữ Nôm texts that translates the "sounds" (word-for-word) of the original Literary Chinese text.[[75]](#cite_note-78)[[76]](#cite_note-79) Examples include [Tam thiên tự giải âm](/wiki/Tam_thi%C3%AAn_t%E1%BB%B1 "Tam thiên tự") (三千字解音), etc. Often these translations attempt to match the word order as the original Literary Chinese text with no regard for Vietnamese syntax). There is significant diversity within works called *giải âm* (even within a single text), with some coming fairly close to the modern concept of translation, while some are closer to annotations with "word-matching" between Literary Chinese and Vietnamese, with little to no regard to the structure or syntax of Vietnamese.

Here is a line in *Tam tự kinh lục bát diễn âm* (三字經六八演音), a Vietnamese translation of the [Three Character Classic](/wiki/Three_Character_Classic "Three Character Classic"). It features the original text on the top of the page and the Vietnamese translation on the bottom.

> 人不𭓇不知理 (*Nhân bất học bất tri lý*)
>
> 𠊚空𭓇別𨤰夷麻推 (*Người không học biết nhẽ gì mà suy*)
>
> Without learning, one does not understand reason.[[d]](#cite_note-80)

* *Diễn âm* (演音) - synonym of *giải âm* (解音).

* *Giải nghĩa* (解義) – a category of chữ Nôm texts that translates the "meaning", often having no regard for Literary Chinese syntax.[[77]](#cite_note-:0-81) Examples include *Chỉ nam ngọc âm giải nghĩa* (指南玉音解義).[[77]](#cite_note-:0-81) Some *giải nghĩa or diễn nghĩa* works focus on extracting as much meaning as possible from the source texts, with little to no regard to the structure and syntax of Literary Chinese.
* *Diễn nghĩa* (演義) – synonym of *giải nghĩa* (解義).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Second_page_of_Tam_t%E1%BB%B1_kinh_l%E1%BB%A5c_b%C3%A1t_di%E1%BB%85n_%C3%A2m_%E4%B8%89%E5%AD%97%E7%B6%93%E5%85%AD%E5%85%AB%E6%BC%94%E9%9F%B3.jpg/250px-Second_page_of_Tam_t%E1%BB%B1_kinh_l%E1%BB%A5c_b%C3%A1t_di%E1%BB%85n_%C3%A2m_%E4%B8%89%E5%AD%97%E7%B6%93%E5%85%AD%E5%85%AB%E6%BC%94%E9%9F%B3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Second_page_of_Tam_t%E1%BB%B1_kinh_l%E1%BB%A5c_b%C3%A1t_di%E1%BB%85n_%C3%A2m_%E4%B8%89%E5%AD%97%E7%B6%93%E5%85%AD%E5%85%AB%E6%BC%94%E9%9F%B3.jpg)

The second page of *Tam tự kinh lục bát diễn âm* (三字經六八演音) with the original Literary Chinese text on the top, and Vietnamese translation on the bottom. The Vietnamese text is written in chữ Nôm and [lục bát](/wiki/L%E1%BB%A5c_b%C3%A1t "Lục bát") (六八) verse form.

## Characters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Characters")]

Vietnamese is a [tonal language](/wiki/Tone_(linguistics) "Tone (linguistics)"), as is Chinese, and has nearly 5,000 distinct syllables.[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199778–79,_82-28)
In chữ Nôm, each monosyllabic word of Vietnamese was represented by a character, either borrowed from Chinese or locally created.
The resulting system was even more difficult to use than the Chinese script.[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarr1984141-30)

As an [analytic language](/wiki/Analytic_language "Analytic language"), Vietnamese was a better fit for a character-based script than Japanese and Korean, with their [agglutinative](/wiki/Agglutinative "Agglutinative") morphology.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarr1984142-60)
Partly for this reason, there was no development of a phonetic system that could be taught to the general public, like Japanese [kana](/wiki/Kana "Kana") syllabary or the Korean [hangul](/wiki/Hangul "Hangul") alphabet, until the arrival of the Europeans.[[78]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarr1984141–142-82)
Moreover, most Vietnamese literati viewed Chinese as the proper medium of civilized writing, and had no interest in turning Nôm into a form of writing suitable for mass communication.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarr1984142-60)

### Variant characters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Variant characters")]

Chữ Nôm has never been standardized.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel2019153-83) As a result, a Vietnamese word could be represented by several Nôm characters. For example, the very word *chữ* ('character', 'script'), a Chinese loanword, can be written as either [字](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%AD%97 "wikt:字") (Chinese character), 𡦂 (Vietnamese-only compound-semantic character) or 𡨸 (Vietnamese-only semantic-phonetic character). For another example, the word *giữa* ('middle'; 'in between') can be written either as 𡨌 (⿰守中) or 𫡉 (⿰字中). Both characters were invented for Vietnamese and have a semantic-phonetic structure, the difference being the phonetic indicator ([守](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%AE%88 "wikt:守") vs. [字](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%AD%97 "wikt:字")).

Another example of a Vietnamese word that is represented by several Nôm characters is the word for moon, *trăng*. It can be represented by a Chinese character that is phonetically similar to trăng, 菱 (lăng), a Nôm character, 𢁋 (⿱巴陵) which is composed of two phonetic components 巴 (ba) and 陵 (lăng) for the Middle Vietnamese *blăng*, or a chữ Nôm character, 𦝄 (⿰月夌) composed of a phonetic component 夌 (lăng) and a semantic component meaning 月 ('moon').

### Borrowed characters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Borrowed characters")]

See also: [Sino-Vietnamese vocabulary](/wiki/Sino-Vietnamese_vocabulary "Sino-Vietnamese vocabulary") and [Literary and colloquial readings of Chinese characters](/wiki/Literary_and_colloquial_readings_of_Chinese_characters "Literary and colloquial readings of Chinese characters")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/a5/C%C3%A2n_kh%C4%83n.png)](/wiki/File:C%C3%A2n_kh%C4%83n.png)

Characters for *cân* (top) and *khăn* (bottom), meaning turban/towel, in *Tự Đức thánh chế tự học giải nghĩa ca.* The character for *khăn* has a diacritic to indicate different pronunciation.

Unmodified Chinese characters were used in chữ Nôm in three different ways.

* A large proportion of Vietnamese vocabulary had been borrowed from Chinese during the Tang period. Such [Sino-Vietnamese vocabulary](/wiki/Sino-Vietnamese_vocabulary "Sino-Vietnamese vocabulary") could be written with the original Chinese character for each word, for example:[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199780–81-84)
  + [役](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%BD%B9 "wikt:役") *dịch* ziːk˨˩ ('service', 'corvée'), from Early [Middle Chinese](/wiki/Middle_Chinese "Middle Chinese") (EMC) /jwiajk/[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPulleyblank1991371-85)
  + [本](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%9C%AC "wikt:本") *bản* ɓaːn˧˨˧ ('root', 'foundation'), from EMC /pənˀ/[[82]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPulleyblank199132-86)
  + [頭](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%A0%AD "wikt:頭") *đầu* dɤ̆ːw˨˩ ('head'), from EMC /dəw/[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPulleyblank1991311-87)
* One way to represent a native Vietnamese word was to use a Chinese character for a Chinese word with a similar meaning. For example, 本 may also represent *vốn* ('capital, funds'). In this case, the word *vốn* is actually an earlier Chinese loan that has become accepted as Vietnamese; William Hannas claims that all such readings are similar early loans.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199780–81-84)
* Alternatively, a native Vietnamese word could be written using a Chinese character for a Chinese word with a similar sound, regardless of the meaning of the Chinese word. For example, [沒](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%B2%92 "wikt:沒") (Early Middle Chinese /mət/[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPulleyblank1991218-88)) may represent the Vietnamese word *một* ('one').[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199780-89)

The first two categories are similar to the *[on](/wiki/On%27yomi "On'yomi")* and [*kun* readings](/wiki/Kun%27yomi "Kun'yomi") of Japanese [kanji](/wiki/Kanji#Readings "Kanji") respectively.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199780-89) The third is similar to *[ateji](/wiki/Ateji "Ateji")*, in which characters are used only for their sound value, or the [Man'yōgana](/wiki/Man%27y%C5%8Dgana "Man'yōgana") script that became the origin of [hiragana](/wiki/Hiragana "Hiragana") and [katakana](/wiki/Katakana "Katakana").

When a character would have two readings, a diacritic may be added to the character to indicate the "indigenous" reading. The two most common alternate reading diacritical marks are *cá* (𖿰), (a variant form of 个) and *nháy* (𖿱).[[86]](#cite_note-90) Thus when 本 is meant to be read as *vốn*, it is written as 本𖿱,[[e]](#cite_note-91) with a diacritic at the upper right corner.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199781-92)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Nh%C3%A1yExample.png/250px-Nh%C3%A1yExample.png)](/wiki/File:Nh%C3%A1yExample.png)

Here the character tự 自 is written with the nháy mark (𖿱), showing to use its alternative reading từ or vice versa. (Depending on the context, the alternative reading could also be tợ instead).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Th%C3%A1u_%C4%91%E1%BA%A5m_%E5%9C%8B.png/250px-Th%C3%A1u_%C4%91%E1%BA%A5m_%E5%9C%8B.png)](/wiki/File:Th%C3%A1u_%C4%91%E1%BA%A5m_%E5%9C%8B.png)

An example of what *tháu đấm* would be with the character *quốc* (國) which was written as 王 with two dots on its left and right side

Other alternate reading diacritical marks include *tháu đấm* (草𢶸) where a character is represented by a simplified variant with two points on either side of the character.[[88]](#cite_note-93)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Activities_of_Tonkin_3.png/250px-Activities_of_Tonkin_3.png)](/wiki/File:Activities_of_Tonkin_3.png)

Usage of diacritical mark nháy (𖿱) can be seen here. The character *yết* (揭) with the mark ' 𖿱 ' changes the reading to xiết 揭𖿱 (Middle left). For *lâm* (林) with the mark ' 𖿱 ' changes the reading to chấm 林𖿱 (Middle right).

### Locally invented characters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Locally invented characters")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Ng%C6%B0%E1%BB%9Di_Vi%E1%BB%87t_%F0%A0%8A%9B%E8%B6%8A.png/250px-Ng%C6%B0%E1%BB%9Di_Vi%E1%BB%87t_%F0%A0%8A%9B%E8%B6%8A.png)](/wiki/File:Ng%C6%B0%E1%BB%9Di_Vi%E1%BB%87t_%F0%A0%8A%9B%E8%B6%8A.png)

The Nôm character for *người* ([𠊛](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%F0%A0%8A%9B "wikt:𠊛")), a term for people or humans (in general). The radical [㝵](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E3%9D%B5 "wikt:㝵") on the left suggests that the pronunciation of the character is linked to that of 㝵 ngại. The radical [人](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%BA%BA "wikt:人") on the right suggests that the meaning of the character is linked to people. (The character after is the word *Việt* 越 meaning "Vietnamese"). The two characters mean "Vietnamese people".

In contrast to the few hundred Japanese [kokuji](/wiki/Kokuji "Kokuji") (国字) and handful of Korean [gukja](/wiki/Gukja "Gukja") (국자, 國字), which are mostly rarely used characters for indigenous natural phenomena, Vietnamese scribes created thousands of new characters, used throughout the language.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199779-94)

As in the Chinese writing system, the most common kind of invented character in Nôm is the phono-semantic compound, made by combining two characters or components, one suggesting the word's meaning and the other its approximate sound. For example:[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199781-92)

* [𠀧](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%F0%A0%80%A7 "wikt:𠀧") (*ba* 'three') is composed of the phonetic part [巴](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%B7%B4 "wikt:巴") (Sino-Vietnamese reading: *ba*) and the semantic part [三](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B8%89 "wikt:三") 'three'. 'Father' is also *ba*, but written as [爸](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%88%B8 "wikt:爸") (⿱[父](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%88%B6 "wikt:父")[巴](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%B7%B4 "wikt:巴")), while 'turtle' is *con ba ba* [𡥵](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%F0%A1%A5%B5 "wikt:𡥵")[蚆](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%9A%86 "wikt:蚆")[蚆](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%9A%86 "wikt:蚆").
* [媄](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%AA%84 "wikt:媄") (*mẹ* 'mother') has [女](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A5%B3 "wikt:女") 'woman' as semantic component and [美](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E7%BE%8E "wikt:美") (Sino-Vietnamese reading: *mỹ*) as phonetic component.[[f]](#cite_note-95)

A smaller group consists of semantic compound characters, which are composed of two Chinese characters representing words of similar meaning. For example, [𡗶](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%F0%A1%97%B6 "wikt:𡗶") (*giời* or *trời* 'sky', 'heaven') is composed of [天](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E5%A4%A9 "wikt:天") ('sky') and [上](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E4%B8%8A "wikt:上") ('upper').[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHannas199781-92)[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi2020103-96)

A few characters were obtained by modifying Chinese characters related either semantically or phonetically to the word to be represented. For example,

* the Nôm character [𧘇](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%F0%A7%98%87 "wikt:𧘇") (*ấy* 'that', 'those') is a simplified form of the Chinese character [衣](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E8%A1%A3 "wikt:衣") (Sino-Vietnamese reading: *ý*).[[91]](#cite_note-97)
* the Nôm character [𫜵](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%F0%AB%9C%B5 "wikt:𫜵") (*làm* 'work', 'labour') is a simplified form of the Chinese character [濫](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%BF%AB "wikt:濫") (Sino-Vietnamese reading: *lạm*) ([濫](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%BF%AB "wikt:濫") > [𪵯](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%F0%AA%B5%AF "wikt:𪵯") > [𫜵](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%F0%AB%9C%B5 "wikt:𫜵")).[[92]](#cite_note-98)
* the Nôm character [𠬠](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%F0%A0%AC%A0 "wikt:𠬠") (một 'one') comes from the right part of the Chinese character [没](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E6%B2%A1 "wikt:没") (Sino-Vietnamese reading: *một*).[[93]](#cite_note-99)

### Example

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Example")]

As an example of the way chữ Nôm was used to record Vietnamese, the first two lines of the *[Tale of Kiều](/wiki/Tale_of_Kieu "Tale of Kieu")* (1871 edition), written in the traditional [six-eight form](/wiki/L%E1%BB%A5c_b%C3%A1t "Lục bát") of Vietnamese verse, consist of 14 characters:[[94]](#cite_note-100)

year

𠊛

người

person

些

ta,

our

𤾓 𢆥 𥪞 𡎝 𠊛 些

Trăm năm trong cõi người ta,

hundred year in world person our

A hundred years—in this life span on earth,

𡨸

Chữ

才

talent

𡨸

chữ

word

命

𡨸 才 𡨸 命 窖 𱺵 恄 饒

Chữ tài chữ mệnh khéo là ghét nhau.

word talent word destiny clever {to be} hate {each other}

talent and destiny are apt to feud.[[95]](#cite_note-101)

## Computer encoding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Computer encoding")]

In 1993, the Vietnamese government released an 8-bit coding standard for alphabetic Vietnamese ([TCVN 5712](/wiki/TCVN_5712 "TCVN 5712"):1993, or VSCII), as well as a 16-bit standard for Nôm (TCVN 5773:1993).[[96]](#cite_note-102) This group of glyphs is referred to as "V0." In 1994, the [Ideographic Rapporteur Group](/wiki/Ideographic_Rapporteur_Group "Ideographic Rapporteur Group") agreed to include Nôm characters as part of [Unicode](/wiki/Unicode "Unicode").[[97]](#cite_note-unicode-103) A revised standard, TCVN 6909:2001, defines 9,299 glyphs.[[98]](#cite_note-Hong-104) About half of these glyphs are specific to Vietnam.[[98]](#cite_note-Hong-104) Nôm characters not already encoded were added to [CJK Unified Ideographs Extension B](/wiki/CJK_Unified_Ideographs_Extension_B "CJK Unified Ideographs Extension B").[[98]](#cite_note-Hong-104) (These characters have five-digit [hexadecimal](/wiki/Hexadecimal "Hexadecimal") code points. The characters that were encoded earlier have four-digit hex.)

Characters were extracted from the following sources:

1. Hoàng Triều Ân, *Tự điển chữ Nôm Tày* [Nôm of the Tay People], 2003.
2. Institute of Linguistics, *Bảng tra chữ Nôm* [Nôm Index], Hanoi, 1976.
3. Nguyễn Quang Hồng, editor, *Tự điển chữ Nôm* [Nôm Dictionary], 2006.
4. Father Trần Văn Kiệm, *Giúp đọc Nôm và Hán Việt* [Help with Nôm and Sino-Vietnamese], 2004.
5. Vũ Văn Kính & Nguyễn Quang Xỷ, *Tự điển chữ Nôm* [Nôm Dictionary], Saigon, 1971.
6. Vũ Văn Kính, *Bảng tra chữ Nôm miền Nam* [Table of Nôm in the South], 1994.
7. Vũ Văn Kính, *Bảng tra chữ Nôm sau thế kỷ XVII* [Table of Nôm After the 17th Century], 1994.
8. Vũ Văn Kính, *Đại tự điển chữ Nôm* [Great Nôm Dictionary], 1999.
9. Nguyễn Văn Huyên, *Góp phần nghiên cứu văn hoá Việt Nam* [Contributions to the Study of Vietnamese Culture], 1995.[[98]](#cite_note-Hong-104)

The V2, V3, and V4 proposals were developed by a group at the Han-Nom Research Institute led by Nguyễn Quang Hồng.[[98]](#cite_note-Hong-104) V4, developed in 2001, includes over 400 ideograms formerly used by the [Tày people](/wiki/Tay_people "Tay people") of northern Vietnam.[[98]](#cite_note-Hong-104) This allows the [Tày language](/wiki/T%C3%A0y_language "Tày language") to get its own registration code.[[98]](#cite_note-Hong-104) V5 is a set of about 900 characters proposed in 2001.[[98]](#cite_note-Hong-104) As these characters were already part of Unicode, the IRG concluded that they could not be edited and no Vietnamese code was added.[[98]](#cite_note-Hong-104) (This is despite the fact that national codes were added retroactively for version 3.0 in 1999.) The Nôm Na Group, led by Ngô Thanh Nhàn, published a set of nearly 20,000 Nôm characters in 2005.[[100]](#cite_note-Ngô-106) This set includes both the characters proposed earlier and a large group of additional characters referred to as "V6".[[98]](#cite_note-Hong-104) These are mainly Han characters from Trần Văn Kiệm's dictionary which were already assigned code points. Character readings were determined manually by Hồng's group, while Nhàn's group developed software for this purpose.[[101]](#cite_note-VNPF-107) The work of the two groups was integrated and published in 2008 as the *Hán Nôm Coded Character Repertoire*.[[101]](#cite_note-VNPF-107)

The characters that do not exist in Chinese have [Sino-Vietnamese](/wiki/Sino-Vietnamese_vocabulary "Sino-Vietnamese vocabulary") readings that are based on the characters given in parentheses. The common character for *càng* (強) contains the radical 虫 (insects).[[102]](#cite_note-kiem-109) This radical is added redundantly to create 𫋙, a rare variation shown in the chart above. The character 𫡯 (*chàu*) is specific to the [Tày people](/wiki/T%C3%A0y_people "Tày people").[[103]](#cite_note-An-110) It has been part of the Unicode standard only since version 8.0 of June 2015, so there is still very little font and [input method](/wiki/Input_method "Input method") support for it. It is a variation of 朝; the corresponding character in Vietnamese.[[104]](#cite_note-NomFoundation-111)

### Software

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Software")]

There are a number of software tools that can produce chữ Nôm characters simply by typing Vietnamese words in chữ quốc ngữ:

* [HanNomIME](https://web.archive.org/web/20060505105536/http://www.viethoc.com/hannom/bango_intro.php), a Windows-based Vietnamese keyboard driver that supports Hán characters and chữ Nôm.
* [Vietnamese Keyboard Set](https://web.archive.org/web/20120618040841/http://herr.atspace.eu/) which enables chữ Nôm and Hán typing on Mac OS X.
* [WinVNKey](https://winvnkey.sourceforge.net/), a Windows-based Vietnamese multilingual keyboard driver that supports typing chữ Nôm in addition to Traditional and Simplified Chinese.
* [Chunom.org Online Editor](http://www.chunom.org/ime/), a browser-based editor for typing chữ Nôm.
* [Bộ gõ Hán Nôm: Phương Viên](https://bogo.hannom.org/), a rime-based IME for typing chữ Nôm.

Other entry methods:

* (in Chinese) [倉頡之友《倉頡平台2012》](http://www.chinesecj.com/forum/viewthread.php?tid=2596&extra=page%3D1) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20131214123307/http://www.chinesecj.com/forum/viewthread.php?tid=2596&extra=page%3D1) 2013-12-14 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine") [Cangjie input method](/wiki/Cangjie_input_method "Cangjie input method") for Windows that allows keyboard entry of all [Unicode CJK characters](/wiki/Han_unification "Han unification") by character shape. Supports over 70,000 characters. Users may add their own characters and character combinations.

### Fonts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ch%E1%BB%AF_N%C3%B4m&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Fonts")]

Fonts with a sufficient coverage of Chữ Nôm characters include *Han-Nom Gothic*, *Han-Nom Minh*, *Han-Nom Ming*, *Han-Nom Kai*, *Nom Na Tong*, *STXiHei* (*Heiti TC*), *MingLiU* plus *MingLiU-ExtB*, *Han Nom A* plus *Han Nom B*, *FZKaiT-Extended* plus *FZKaiT-Extended(SIP)*, and [Mojikyō](/wiki/Mojiky%C5%8D "Mojikyō") fonts which require special software. The following web pages are collections of URLs from which Chữ Nôm–capable fonts can be downloaded:

* *[Fonts for Chu Nom](https://www.chunom.org/pages/fonts/)* on *chunom.org*.
* *[Han-Nom Fonts](http://hannom-rcv.org/font.html#e)* on *hannom-rcv.org*.
